question: Based on Table 1.3-2 (eliminated systems) and Table 5.4-1 (SG/DHRS parameters), does the DHRS adequately replace the eliminated AFW system?

nuscale_ch01_0714.title: DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0: Loss of External Load; Turbine Trip; Loss of Condenser Vacuum; Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve; and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed)II.3.C Values of Parameters Used in Analytical Model - Core Burnup
nuscale_ch01_0714.summary: This section confirms that the analytical models used for evaluating events such as loss of external load, turbine trip, loss of condenser vacuum, main steam isolation valve closure, and steam pressure regulator failure (closed) conform to the regulatory criteria outlined in DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5. Specifically, it states that the values of parameters used in the models—including core burnup—meet the required standards, with no exceptions noted.

nuscale_ch05_0020.title: Steam Generators
nuscale_ch05_0020.summary: The steam generators (SGs) in the NuScale Power Module are integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which also houses other key components such as the pressurizer and safety valves. The SG system includes feedwater and main steam piping, thermal relief valves, and access ports, with the RPV itself forming the outer pressure boundary for the SGs and contributing to the reactor coolant pressure boundary.

nuscale_ch01_0558.title: DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0: Radiation Protection Design FeaturesII.9 RG 8.15
nuscale_ch01_0558.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory guides related to radiation protection design features, as outlined in DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0. It details whether each guide is applicable, not applicable, conforms, or partially conforms, with references to supporting sections and tables for further information. Specifically, RG 8.15 is noted as not applicable to the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the NuScale Power Plant’s major design features and parameters with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It highlights differences in size, output, and system components, showing that NuScale’s design is smaller, simpler, and incorporates unique features such as no external coolant loops and fewer components, while still meeting safety requirements. The comparison is summarized in tables for clarity.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems in the NuScale Power Module, detailing the integration of the reactor, steam generators, pressurizer, safety and ECCS valves, and associated piping within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). It outlines the configuration and boundaries of the steam generator system, emphasizing the integral design of the steam generators within the RPV and the separation between primary and secondary sides.

nuscale_ch01_0128.title: 1.9.3 Generic Issues
nuscale_ch01_0128.summary: This section describes NuScale's approach to ensuring conformance with regulatory requirements, including the Standard Review Plan (SRP), Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS), and NRC Interim Staff Guidance. It outlines how NuScale addresses technically relevant Three Mile Island (TMI) requirements and unresolved or high-priority generic safety issues, referencing the NRC Generic Issues Program and relevant regulatory documents.

nuscale_ch01_0076.title: Conformance with Standard Review Plan
nuscale_ch01_0076.summary: NuScale reviewed the Standard Review Plan (SRP), including relevant guidance and acceptance criteria, to ensure conformance with regulatory requirements, and documented this in a gap analysis report submitted to the NRC. The company also addressed compliance with the Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS) and NRC Interim Staff Guidance, as well as assessed and documented resolutions for applicable Three Mile Island (TMI) requirements and unresolved generic safety issues, as required by federal regulations.

nuscale_ch01_0135.title: Table 1.9-3: Conformance with NUREG-0800, Standard Review Plan (SRP) and Design Specific Review Standard (DSRS)
nuscale_ch01_0135.summary: Table 1.9-3 summarizes NuScale’s conformance with the regulatory criteria outlined in NUREG-0800 (SRP) and DSRS, indicating whether each section’s acceptance criteria are met or applicable. The table shows that NuScale conforms to relevant SRP sections, while certain criteria are marked not applicable as they pertain only to Combined License (COL) applicants not referencing the Design Certification Application (DCA).

nuscale_ch01_0454.title: DSRS 11.1, Rev 0: Coolant Source TermsII.1 RG 1.110
nuscale_ch01_0454.summary: This section explains that several regulatory design guidelines for auxiliary feedwater systems and steam generator configurations are not applicable to the NuScale plant, as its design differs significantly from large pressurized water reactors (PWRs). Specifically, NuScale uses a passive Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) instead of pump-driven auxiliary feedwater systems and does not employ top-feed, preheat, or once-through steam generator designs, thereby avoiding related regulatory concerns such as water hammer.

nuscale_ch01_0449.title: BTP 10-1, Rev 3: Design Guidelines for Auxiliary Feedwater System Pump Drive and Power Supply Diversity for Pressurized Water Reactor PlantsAll Design Guidelines for Auxiliary Feedwater System Pump Drive and Power Supply Diversity
nuscale_ch01_0449.summary: This section explains that the NuScale plant design does not use traditional Auxiliary Feedwater (AFW) systems or steam generator designs found in large pressurized water reactors (PWRs). Instead, NuScale employs a passive Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) that fulfills similar safety functions without pumps or the associated risks, making the referenced regulatory guidelines not applicable.

nuscale_ch01_0019.title: Operating Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0019.summary: The NuScale Power Module (NPM) operates using natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps, and features a small, modular, integral pressurized water reactor design. It relies on passive safety systems—including dedicated emergency core cooling and decay heat removal systems—that do not require electrical power, and its components are housed in steel containment partially immersed in water for effective heat removal. The NPM allows for flexible power maneuvering through control rods and boron concentration adjustments, with all major components integrated to minimize external piping and penetrations.

nuscale_ch01_0669.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.4 Specific CriterionInstrument Spans and Setpoints use RG 1.105
nuscale_ch01_0669.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operating occurrences such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets requirements for system pressure, critical heat flux, event classification, and instrument setpoints, aligning with NRC guidance and ensuring plant safety under these conditions.

nuscale_ch01_0448.title: SRP 10.4.9, Rev 3: Auxiliary Feedwater System (PWR)All Various
nuscale_ch01_0448.summary: The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report states that the NuScale plant design does not require or use an auxiliary feedwater system (AFWS) as described in SRP 10.4.9, Rev 3. Instead, the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) performs some similar functions but differs significantly in design, operation, and its role during small break LOCA events, making the AFWS criteria not applicable to NuScale.

nuscale_ch01_0450.title: BTP 10-2, Rev 4: Design Guidelines for Avoiding Water Hammers in Steam GeneratorsTFSGD B.1 thru B.4Top-Feed Steam Generator Designs
nuscale_ch01_0450.summary: This section explains that the NuScale plant design does not use top-feed, preheat, or once-through steam generator designs, so the regulatory guidelines for avoiding water hammers in these systems are not applicable. Instead, NuScale uses a passive natural circulation system (DHRS) that fulfills a similar function to the auxiliary feedwater system in large PWRs, but its design inherently avoids water hammer issues addressed by the guidelines.

nuscale_ch01_0022.title: Engineered Safety Feature Materials
nuscale_ch01_0022.summary: The section describes how maintaining a vacuum in the NuScale Containment Vessel (CNV) offers several safety and operational benefits, such as reducing corrosion, aiding leak detection, and minimizing combustible gases. The design relies on passive safety features, including passive cooling and the elimination of large external coolant loops, resulting in improved safety, lower core damage frequency, and a smaller radioactive source term compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0664.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.1 Basic ObjectiveIdentify Limiting Increase in Heat Removal Events
nuscale_ch01_0664.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for analyzing limiting increase in heat removal events, such as decreases in feedwater temperature, increases in feedwater or steam flow, and inadvertent operation of turbine bypass or decay heat removal systems. It confirms that the NuScale design meets the objectives of identifying limiting events and verifying that fuel damage and system pressure criteria are satisfied, with no exceptions noted.

nuscale_ch01_0285.title: SRP BTP 8-6, Rev 3: Adequacy of Station Electric Distribution System VoltagesAll Criteria for evaluating voltage protection for the offsite power system to assure proper operation and sequencing of Class 1E loads
nuscale_ch01_0285.summary: This section summarizes NuScale's conformance with various regulatory criteria related to electric power systems, noting that backup diesel generators are used solely for standby power and not interconnected except during testing. It also states that certain criteria, such as grid stability analysis and single failure considerations for manually controlled valves, are not applicable to the NuScale design, as these responsibilities fall to the COL applicant or are not relevant to the system's design.

nuscale_ch01_0857.title: SRP 19.1, Rev 3: Determining The Technical Adequacy of Probabilistic Risk Assessment For Risk-Informed License Amendment Requests After Initial Fuel LoadAll Various
nuscale_ch01_0857.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various NRC regulatory criteria related to human factors engineering and probabilistic risk assessment (PRA) for risk-informed license amendments. It notes that NuScale conforms to the review criteria for crediting manual operator actions in diversity and defense-in-depth analyses, as outlined in SRP Appendix 18-A, but only partially conforms to SRP 19.0 requirements for PRA, as site-specific hazard evaluation and PRA updates are the responsibility of the COL applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety systems and integral design eliminate the need for external power and large coolant loops, significantly lowering the risk of core damage and improving safety compared to traditional nuclear plants.

nuscale_ch01_0663.title: DSRS 15.0.3, Rev 0: Design Basis Accident Radiological Consequence Analyses for the NuScale SMR DesignII.3 Technical Support Center Radiological Habitability
nuscale_ch01_0663.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for radiological consequence analyses during design basis accidents, specifically addressing the Technical Support Center (TSC) radiological habitability. It notes that the TSC meets dose acceptance criteria when AC power is available, but its function is transferred to the main control room if AC power is lost, indicating only partial conformance for TSC habitability under all conditions.

nuscale_ch01_0325.title: SRP 9.2.2, Rev 4: Reactor Auxiliary Cooling Water SystemII.5 Cooling Water System Inspection
nuscale_ch01_0325.summary: The section states that the NuScale Reactor Auxiliary Cooling Water System does not serve a safety-related cooling or accident mitigation function, so the inspection requirements of SRP 9.2.2, II.5 are not applicable. This is consistent with previous acceptance criteria in the document, which also found these requirements not applicable due to the system's nonsafety-related classification.

nuscale_ch01_0662.title: DSRS 15.0.3, Rev 0: Design Basis Accident Radiological Consequence Analyses for the NuScale SMR DesignII.2 Control Room Radiological Habitability
nuscale_ch01_0662.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s compliance with regulatory criteria for radiological consequence analyses during design basis accidents, specifically addressing control room radiological habitability. It confirms that the NuScale SMR design meets the relevant requirements of DSRS 15.0.3, ensuring adequate protection for control room personnel during postulated accidents. The section also references conformance tables that map regulatory standards to the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0352.title: SRP 9.3.2, Rev 3: Process and Post-Accident Sampling SystemsII.3 Process Sampling System Functional Design
nuscale_ch01_0352.summary: The section reviews NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for process and post-accident sampling systems, noting that most requirements are met except for those not applicable to the NuScale design or addressed by prior NRC actions. Specifically, the process sampling system’s functional design and seismic/quality classifications conform, while some criteria only partially apply due to design differences. Additionally, related systems such as equipment drainage and chemical/volume control are evaluated, with most requirements met except where only specific safety functions are relevant.

nuscale_ch01_0324.title: SRP 9.2.2, Rev 4: Reactor Auxiliary Cooling Water SystemII.4 Cooling Water System
nuscale_ch01_0324.summary: This section explains that the NuScale reactor component cooling water (RCCW) system is not classified as safety-related and therefore does not fully conform to certain regulatory criteria for safety-related systems. Specifically, the RCCW system is designed to Seismic Category II standards for structures whose failure could impact safety-related systems, but many requirements, including those for cooling water system inspection and sharing, are deemed not applicable.

nuscale_ch01_0357.title: DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0: Chemical and Volume Control System (PWR) (Including Boron Recovery System)II.1 CVCS Functional Performance during Adverse Environmental Phenomena; Pumping Capacity; and defense-in-depth RCS makeup
nuscale_ch01_0357.summary: The section reviews NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria for the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS), focusing on its performance during adverse environmental conditions, pumping capacity, and defense-in-depth for reactor coolant system (RCS) makeup. It notes that the CVCS partially conforms, as its only safety-related function is to prevent inadvertent boron dilution, and that single-failure criteria apply solely to the two safety-related demineralized water isolation valves. Other related systems and criteria are also addressed, with most either conforming or being not applicable to the NuScale design.

edges.description: Section B provides the design parameters and functional description of the DHRS and steam generators, which are referenced in Section A's analytical models to justify the elimination of the AFW system and demonstrate regulatory conformance.

Section A discusses the analytical model and parameters used for evaluating system responses to certain transients, while Section B provides a comparison of NuScale's design features and parameters (including those in Table 1.3-2 and 5.4-1) with typical PWRs, thus Section B provides the context and baseline for interpreting the adequacy of DHRS as a replacement for AFW as analyzed in Section A.

Section B describes the design and parameters of the DHRS, which is relevant to Section A's analysis of system responses to transients where the AFW system has been eliminated, thus connecting the adequacy of DHRS as a replacement for AFW in meeting regulatory requirements.

Section A provides the analytical model parameters and conformance for specific transient events, while Section B discusses the overall conformance with regulatory review plans and acceptance criteria, connecting them through the evaluation of whether the DHRS system (analyzed in A) meets the regulatory requirements and standards referenced in B.

Section A provides the analytical model parameters and conformance details for specific transient events, while Section B documents the overall conformance with the Standard Review Plan and acceptance criteria, connecting them through the regulatory requirement that the analytical models (A) must satisfy the SRP criteria reviewed in B.

Section A describes the analytical models and parameter values used to demonstrate conformance with DSRS requirements for transient events, while Section B documents the overall conformance status with NUREG-0800 and DSRS acceptance criteria, thus Section A provides supporting evidence for the claims of regulatory conformance summarized in Section B.

Section A describes the design and components of the NuScale module, including the DHRS and steam generators, while Section B provides a comparison table that lists eliminated systems (such as AFW) and their NuScale replacements, connecting the DHRS as the functional replacement for AFW based on design parameters.

Section A describes the design and components of the reactor coolant system, including the DHRS and steam generators, while Section B discusses the process of reviewing regulatory criteria and acceptance standards, which would include evaluating whether the DHRS meets requirements previously satisfied by the eliminated AFW system.

Section A describes the design and components of the NuScale module, including the DHRS and steam generators, while Section B discusses the review of regulatory criteria and acceptance standards that would apply to evaluating whether the DHRS can replace the eliminated AFW system.

Section A describes the design and components of the reactor coolant system, including the DHRS and steam generators, while Section B documents the system's conformance with regulatory criteria, thus Section A provides the technical basis that supports the regulatory claims made in Section B regarding system adequacy.

Section B provides comparative design parameters (including for DHRS and AFW) that can be used to assess whether the DHRS in NuScale adequately replaces the eliminated AFW system listed in Section A's Table 1.3-2, thus connecting the eliminated system (A) with the replacement system's parameters (B).

Section B describes the design and parameters of the DHRS and steam generators, which are referenced in Table 1.3-2 (eliminated systems) as the replacement for the eliminated AFW system, thus providing the technical basis to assess whether the DHRS adequately replaces the AFW system as required by the analysis question.

Section A lists specific regulatory guidance documents and the degree of conformance for radiation protection design features, while Section B describes a broader gap analysis process used to assess conformance with the Standard Review Plan (SRP) and its acceptance criteria, indicating that the details in Section A are specific instances or results of the general process described in Section B.

Section A lists specific regulatory guidance documents and the degree of conformance for radiation protection design features, while Section B describes the overall process and results of the NuScale FSAR's conformance review with the Standard Review Plan, connecting them through the evaluation of regulatory compliance.

Section A references specific DSRS and RG criteria for radiation protection design features, while Section B provides a summary table of conformance with those same regulatory criteria, connecting them through their shared focus on regulatory compliance and cross-referencing of requirements.

Section A lists eliminated systems (such as AFW) and provides context for design changes, while Section B details the parameters and design of the DHRS and SG, allowing for a direct comparison to assess whether DHRS can functionally replace the eliminated AFW system.

Section A provides comparative design information relevant to system eliminations, while Section B discusses regulatory conformance and gap analysis, connecting them through the evaluation of whether the DHRS meets regulatory requirements after AFW elimination.

Section A provides a comparison of NuScale's design features and parameters with those of a typical PWR, while Section B discusses how NuScale's design (including such features) conforms to regulatory criteria, thus Section A's information supports the regulatory conformance claims made in Section B.

Section A provides a comparison of NuScale's design features and parameters with those of a typical PWR, while Section B documents the plant's conformance with regulatory criteria, connecting them through the evaluation of whether design changes (such as eliminating AFW and relying on DHRS) meet regulatory requirements.

Section A describes the design and components of the reactor coolant system, including the DHRS, while Section B discusses the conformance of these systems (including DHRS) with regulatory criteria, connecting the design features to regulatory requirements for system adequacy.

Section A describes the design and components of the DHRS as part of the reactor coolant system, while Section B discusses the overall conformance of the design (including DHRS) with regulatory criteria, connecting them through the regulatory evaluation of whether DHRS can replace the eliminated AFW system.

Section A describes the design and components of the DHRS as part of the reactor coolant system, while Section B documents the system's conformance with regulatory criteria, thus Section A provides the technical basis that supports the regulatory claims made in Section B regarding system adequacy.

Section A describes the process and results of NuScale's review for conformance with regulatory criteria, while Section B provides a tabulated summary of how the design conforms to specific SRP and DSRS sections, with Section B serving as detailed evidence supporting the claims made in Section A.

Section A describes the process and documentation of NuScale's review for conformance with the Standard Review Plan (SRP), while Section B provides a tabular summary of the actual conformance status for each SRP section, thus Section B specifies the detailed results referenced in Section A's general description.

Section A states that the NuScale DHRS is intended to fulfill a similar safety function as the eliminated AFW system, while Section B provides the analytical parameters and accident scenarios used to evaluate system performance, thus connecting the DHRS's functional adequacy (from A) to its performance in relevant safety analyses (from B).

Section A states that the NuScale DHRS fulfills a similar function as the eliminated AFW system, while Section B describes the DHRS and SGs as integrated components of the reactor coolant system, thus connecting the functional replacement claim with the technical design details.

Section A explains that the NuScale DHRS is intended to fulfill a similar safety function as the eliminated AFW system, while Section B provides the analytical criteria and parameter values used to evaluate system performance during relevant transients, thus connecting the DHRS's functional adequacy (from A) to the regulatory performance requirements (from B).

Section A states that the NuScale DHRS fulfills a similar function as the eliminated AFW system, while Section B provides the design parameters and integration details of the DHRS and steam generators, allowing assessment of whether the DHRS can adequately replace the AFW system.

Section A explains that the DHRS is intended to fulfill a similar safety function as the eliminated AFW system, while Section B lists regulatory conformance for radiation protection features, which are relevant to evaluating whether the DHRS replacement maintains required safety standards.

Section A describes the NuScale module's reliance on passive safety systems and natural circulation, which eliminates the need for systems like AFW, while Section B references regulatory criteria and analytical models that must be satisfied by the replacement system (DHRS) to ensure safety during transients previously mitigated by AFW.

Section A explains that the NuScale design eliminates the need for auxiliary feedwater (AFW) by relying on passive safety systems and natural circulation, while Section B details the integration of steam generators and the decay heat removal system (DHRS) within the reactor module, showing that DHRS is intended to fulfill the safety function previously provided by AFW.

Section A and Section B both reference analytical models and criteria for transient events, with Section A focusing on events related to feedwater and decay heat removal system operation and Section B on turbine and steam system transients, indicating that both sections use similar regulatory approaches and parameter values to evaluate system adequacy in accident scenarios.

Section B describes the design and parameters of the DHRS and steam generators, which are referenced in Section A as part of the systems analyzed for conformance with regulatory criteria after the elimination of the AFW system, thus connecting the system replacement analysis.

Section A discusses the partial conformance of the NuScale design with regulatory criteria for auxiliary feedwater system requirements, while Section B presents the analytical model and parameters used to evaluate system performance during transients, connecting them through the evaluation of whether the DHRS parameters (Table 5.4-1) satisfy the safety functions previously provided by the eliminated AFW system (Table 1.3-2).

Section A discusses the regulatory requirements and partial conformance for auxiliary feedwater system elimination, while Section B provides the design parameters and components of the DHRS and steam generators that are intended to fulfill the safety functions of the eliminated AFW system, thus connecting the regulatory requirement to the design solution.

Section A explains that the NuScale DHRS is intended to fulfill a similar safety function as the eliminated AFW system, while Section B provides the analytical parameters and regulatory criteria used to evaluate system performance during relevant transients, thus connecting the DHRS's functional adequacy (from A) to the regulatory performance requirements (from B).

Section A states that the NuScale DHRS is intended to fulfill a similar function as the eliminated AFW system, while Section B describes the design and integration of the DHRS and steam generators, providing the technical parameters necessary to assess whether the DHRS can adequately replace the AFW system.

Section A states that the NuScale DHRS fulfills a similar function as the AFW system described in Section B, indicating that the DHRS is intended to replace the eliminated AFW system by meeting the same functional requirements.

Section A states that the NuScale DHRS fulfills a similar function as the eliminated AFW system, while Section B describes the passive safety and natural circulation features of the NuScale design that enable such functional replacement.

Section A explains that the NuScale DHRS is intended to fulfill a similar safety function as the eliminated AFW system, while Section B provides the regulatory criteria and system performance requirements (such as pressure and setpoints) that the DHRS must meet in various transient scenarios, thus connecting the design intent in A with the regulatory performance expectations in B.

Section A explains that the NuScale DHRS is intended to fulfill a similar safety function as the eliminated AFW system described in Section B, connecting them through their roles in post-shutdown heat removal, but notes differences in applicability due to plant design.

Section A states that the NuScale DHRS fulfills a similar function as the eliminated AFW system, while Section B reiterates that the DHRS replaces the AFW system's function in the NuScale design, both referencing the applicability of AFW-related guidelines to DHRS.

Section A states that the NuScale DHRS fulfills a similar function to the eliminated AFW system but is not subject to the same guidelines, while Section B describes the DHRS's passive safety features and natural circulation, providing operational evidence that supports the DHRS as an adequate replacement.

Section A explains that the NuScale DHRS is intended to fulfill a similar safety function as the eliminated AFW system, while Section B provides the regulatory criteria and system performance requirements (such as pressure and setpoints) that the replacement system (DHRS) must satisfy, thus connecting the design intent in A to the regulatory requirements in B.

Section A explains that the NuScale DHRS is intended to fulfill a similar safety function as the eliminated AFW system described in Section B, but notes that the specific regulatory guidelines for AFW systems in large PWRs are not directly applicable to the NuScale design.

Section A states that the DHRS fulfills a similar function as the eliminated AFW system, while Section B reiterates that the guidance for AFW is not applicable to NuScale because DHRS replaces its function, thus Section A supports the claim in Section B regarding functional replacement.

Section A describes the NuScale module's reliance on passive safety systems and natural circulation (eliminating the need for active systems like AFW), while Section B outlines regulatory criteria for system response to feedwater and steam flow events, connecting them through the need for the DHRS to meet these regulatory requirements in the absence of AFW.

Section A describes the NuScale plant's reliance on passive safety systems and natural circulation, which eliminates the need for systems like the AFW, while Section B discusses regulatory conformance and the applicability of AFW-related criteria to the NuScale design, connecting them through the design's approach to replacing AFW functions with DHRS.

Section B states that the NuScale DHRS fulfills a similar function as the eliminated AFW system, directly connecting the DHRS's role to the regulatory requirements for AFW, while Section A describes the passive safety and natural circulation features that enable this replacement.

Section A discusses the regulatory criteria and safety analysis for events involving the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), while Section B outlines the regulatory requirements and acceptance criteria for the Auxiliary Feedwater (AFW) system, with both sections connected by the need to demonstrate that the DHRS can fulfill the safety functions previously provided by the eliminated AFW system in accordance with regulatory expectations.

Section B states that the NuScale DHRS fulfills a similar function as the eliminated AFW system, directly supporting the analysis of whether DHRS can adequately replace AFW as referenced in Section A's regulatory conformance discussion.

Section B states that the NuScale DHRS fulfills a similar function as the eliminated AFW system referenced in Section A, indicating that the DHRS is intended to replace the AFW system's role in the plant design.

Section A describes the benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which is a design feature that may impact system reliability and safety, while Section B lists the analytical models and parameters (including loss of condenser vacuum) used to demonstrate conformance with regulatory criteria, connecting them through the operational and safety implications of vacuum conditions in accident analyses.

Section A establishes the regulatory objectives and accident scenarios (including those affected by the elimination of the AFW system), while Section B provides the analytical models and parameter values (such as those for DHRS performance) used to demonstrate that the DHRS can adequately address these scenarios, thus connecting the design's capability to regulatory requirements.

Section A establishes the regulatory objectives and accident analysis criteria that must be met for heat removal systems, while Section B describes the design features and parameters of the DHRS and steam generators that are intended to fulfill those regulatory requirements after the AFW system is eliminated.

Section A addresses the design basis accident analyses and system eliminations relevant to heat removal and radiological consequences, while Section B details radiation protection design features and regulatory guidance conformance, both of which are connected through their focus on ensuring radiological safety in the context of system changes such as replacing AFW with DHRS.

Section A specifies regulatory criteria and conformance for electric power systems, while Section B specifies analytical model parameters for transient events; both are connected through the requirement that safety systems (like DHRS) must function adequately under loss-of-power scenarios, which is relevant to evaluating if DHRS can replace AFW.

Section B provides the design details and parameters of the DHRS and steam generators, which are necessary to evaluate whether the DHRS can adequately replace the eliminated AFW system listed in Section A's Table 1.3-2.

Section A references regulatory criteria for evaluating the adequacy of risk-informed changes (such as system eliminations), while Section B specifies the analytical parameters and models used to assess system performance under transient conditions, thus Section B provides the technical basis needed to determine if the DHRS can adequately replace the eliminated AFW system as required by the regulatory framework in Section A.

Section B provides the design parameters and functional description of the DHRS and steam generators, which are necessary to evaluate whether the DHRS can adequately replace the eliminated AFW system as listed in Section A's Table 1.3-2.

Section A describes the safety benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel, which is relevant to the elimination of certain systems, while Section B outlines the regulatory criteria and analytical modeling requirements for transient events, connecting them through the need to demonstrate that the new design (including DHRS) meets safety and regulatory expectations after AFW elimination.

Section B describes the integrated design of the steam generators and DHRS within the reactor pressure vessel, which is relevant to Section A's discussion of safety considerations for the containment vessel, connecting them through the design features that support the elimination of the AFW system.

Section A describes engineered safety feature materials and containment vacuum benefits, while Section B outlines regulatory criteria for accident analyses and conformance, with both sections relating through the design's ability to meet regulatory safety objectives after system changes such as AFW elimination.

Section B provides regulatory criteria and acceptance standards for evaluating plant modifications and risk-important systems, which are necessary to judge whether the DHRS (Section A's context) adequately replaces the eliminated AFW system as per the analysis question.

Section A and Section B are identical text excerpts describing the benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV, with both sections providing the same information about engineered safety feature materials and safety considerations.

Section A discusses the regulatory objectives and conformance for accident analyses and system eliminations, while Section B addresses the technical adequacy of risk assessments and human factors for plant modifications, with both sections connected through their evaluation of system changes (such as the elimination of AFW and adequacy of DHRS) in meeting safety and regulatory requirements.

Section A discusses regulatory objectives and accident analyses relevant to the elimination of the AFW system, while Section B describes the design features of the NuScale containment (CNV) that support alternative heat removal strategies, connecting them through the evaluation of whether the DHRS can fulfill the safety functions previously provided by the AFW system.

Section A discusses the conformance of the NuScale design with regulatory criteria for electric power systems, while Section B addresses the adequacy of probabilistic risk assessment and human factors for risk-informed changes, with both sections referencing the evaluation of system adequacy and regulatory compliance for safety-related systems, thus connecting through the assessment of whether design changes (like replacing AFW with DHRS) meet regulatory and risk requirements.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and review processes for evaluating technical adequacy and human factors in system modifications, which provide the framework for assessing whether the DHRS can adequately replace the eliminated AFW system as described in Section B's general plant safety considerations.

Section A establishes the radiological consequence analysis requirements for design basis accidents, while Section B provides the analytical model parameters (such as core burnup and initial power) used to evaluate system performance during transients, thus Section B provides supporting evidence and technical detail necessary to demonstrate that the DHRS can satisfy the regulatory requirements outlined in Section A for replacing the AFW system.

Section B provides the technical design details and parameters of the DHRS and steam generators, which are referenced in Section A's radiological consequence analyses to demonstrate that the DHRS can adequately replace the eliminated AFW system in meeting regulatory safety requirements.

Section A addresses radiological consequence analyses for design basis accidents, while Section B discusses radiation protection design features, both of which are connected through their focus on ensuring radiological safety and regulatory conformance in the NuScale SMR design.

Section A discusses the regulatory conformance and safety-related functions of cooling water systems, while Section B outlines the analytical models and parameters used to evaluate system performance during transient events, connecting them through the evaluation of whether the DHRS parameters (Table 5.4-1) satisfy the safety functions previously provided by the eliminated AFW system (Table 1.3-2).

Section A lists the AFW system as eliminated and references the regulatory context for cooling water systems, while Section B provides the design parameters and functional description of the DHRS and steam generators, allowing a comparison to determine if DHRS fulfills the safety and cooling functions previously provided by the AFW system.

Section B describes the design and parameters of the DHRS and steam generators, which are referenced in Section A's radiological consequence analyses to demonstrate conformance with regulatory criteria after the elimination of the AFW system.

Section A discusses the radiological consequence analyses and regulatory conformance for accident scenarios, which depend on the adequacy of safety systems like DHRS to replace eliminated systems such as AFW, while Section B addresses the process and post-accident sampling systems that provide supporting data for evaluating such safety system performance; thus, Section B supports the claims in Section A regarding system adequacy.
